examples.title: Binary Search - O(log n)
examples.description: Demonstrates logarithmic time complexity by repeatedly halving the search space
examples.code:
ALGORITHM BinarySearch(arr, target, low, high)
BEGIN
    IF low > high THEN
        RETURN -1  // Not found
    ENDIF
    
    mid = (low + high) / 2
    
    IF arr[mid] == target THEN
        RETURN mid
    ELSE IF arr[mid] < target THEN
        RETURN BinarySearch(arr, target, mid + 1, high)
    ELSE
        RETURN BinarySearch(arr, target, low, mid - 1)
    ENDIF
END

// Recurrence: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1)
// Solution: T(n) = O(log n)
examples.explanation: Each recursive call halves the search space. The recurrence T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) solves to O(log n) by Master Theorem Case 2.

examples.title: Merge Sort Implementation
examples.description: Classic divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm with O(n log n) complexity
examples.explanation: The array is divided into two halves (2 subproblems of size n/2), recursively sorted, then merged in O(n) time. This gives recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n, solving to Θ(n log n).

examples.title: Nested Loops - Quadratic Time
examples.description: Demonstrates O(n²) complexity through nested iteration
examples.explanation: The outer loop runs n times, and for each iteration, the inner loop also runs n times, giving n × n = n² total operations. This is quadratic time complexity.

examples.title: Dynamic Array Amortized Analysis
examples.description: Shows how amortized analysis works for dynamic array resizing
examples.explanation: While individual resize operations cost O(n), they happen infrequently (only when size reaches powers of 2). Using amortized analysis, the average cost per insertion is O(1), since the expensive operations are offset by many cheap ones.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) amortized per operation, O(n) worst case
